speaker: interviewer
text: At least, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --just let her finish the sentence. I'll ask you to say, 'tape two.'

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say, 'tape 2 with Betty Daniel.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you please talk a little bit louder--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when you talk to ask me my question?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two with Betty Daniel. Betty, we were talking about the man who told you about Rosh Hashanah. How did you deal with your religion when you were in Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to try and track the Jewish holidays and celebrate them?

speaker: interviewee
text: At camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew about them. And I don't remember whether we were praying or anything. But we knew. Yeah, we knew. And some-- maybe some of them had some prayer books that could preserve. Because the camps that we were in Neusalz, there were some that came previous. And they could have some personal belongings. They could have some.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from them, maybe they knew. But I personally did not observe, I would say. We didn't-- we went to work. They didn't-- I think the Sundays were off. I'm not sure. I don't remember this. I never thought of that. Yeah, but observing Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, all this here, I don't think whether we observed it. Were you angry with God, yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I don't-- I see as I'm growing older, I see I-- I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- then, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then. Oh, then, no. No, I was not-- not-- I was not angry, no. I tried to-- I tried to be optimistic. I tried to-- oh, I gonna survive. I gonna do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, when you were in Neusalz, did you at any time experience any violence, any punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, unfortunately the human race is an animal, and there were some who abused their power. And if one went to steal from someone else a piece of bread, which was very common in a camp. If you don't eat it, you try to save it for tomorrow. And somebody comes and steal it, you get mad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I I personally did not see it. But I recall that there were some punishments-- cutting off hair, shaving off. And-- Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that? Did you see any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw this, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was one incident in our camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, because I think that she stole the-- a potato or for a very minor thing. And treatment from the one who had the power-- I would say the supervisor. Maybe, I see-- I like to interpret myself that she had do her duties. But sometimes she didn't have to do so much, that she could overlook. She didn't have to see it. But the power, if you have, you abuse it. And you harm someone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes she did abuse her power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were-- there were some incidents. Maybe they were in other camps more vividly, more visible than I. And I personally, because I had the-- I was fortunate to-- to be better than any other one, to have some extras. So this, maybe I-- I-- as I see it now, I tried to be content and satisfied and count my blessings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what was your sister doing at the camp while you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister had a different kind of work. She was at work, I think was the cotton that when you pick up the cotton-- and there were some that worked in the fields, cotton fields. That was very dusty and dirty. My work was cleaner. And my sister's was a little different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your work for us in more detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my work was to supply to the-- to the division where the thread goes from smaller spools-- from bigger spools into smaller spools. It goes through the wooden [? waltzes. ?] And if the [? waltz, ?] the wooden [? waltz ?] is a little chipped, the thread breaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my work was to go in to feel with my hands whether it's smooth or whether it's chipped. If it was chipped, I had to replace the-- the [? waltz, ?] the ones that operates around and goes into the spools. So that was my work. And bringing in the [? waltzes ?] from-- from the French-- the other department-- to supply and to change those here. What-- what did you eat at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't hear you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I eat?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food like, was very-- I think it was nutritious. One-- I recall very well tapioca. Tapioca, as I see now, it was nutrition. Potatoes-- we peeled the potato. We hate the potatoes that time because they cooked them when it was the peels. And now we hear that the peels are vitamins. And we threw them away. Bread. I think there was some-- when it was a holiday, we were treated some-- I would say maybe some dessert, yeah. And the Neusalz-- in my-- in my opinion, Neusalz was nutrition. It was very well handled, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were women treated any differently there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were only women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only women there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only women in the-- in the camp, yes. Only women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in the factory, only women, where you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory was only women, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except the other divisions where the prisoners of war, the French one works. But in this one was only women, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the hygiene for women in Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could shower. We could keep clean, yes. We had clothes. We can wash it, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, do you recall what you dreamt about at night during the time that you spent, the years you spent in Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I recollected that. Very little, very little. Yeah, it's sort of like a blackout.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any conversations with your sister that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no, nothing personal. Nothing. No, not that I recollect, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you spend in Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spent in Neusalz, if counting from February '43-- count end of February, March '43. March '43 till end of '44 or beginning of '45. We started to evacuate because we heard that the Russians are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of 1945, correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was January?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wintertime.

speaker: interviewee
text: January, wintertime. So we started-- so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- so can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the whole-- the whole camp evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were marching, evacuating, going away, running away from the Russians, on foot, wooden shoes. This was close to the Czechoslovakia, which I recall that there was a well-known resort, Carlsbad and Marienbad. Knowing from way back that some of the family went for a vacation. So we recollect that we walked through Carlsbad and Marienbad in very bad condition, with wooden shoes and snow. And very sympathetic, I have to say, people, that they saw us walking and offering us food. Sometimes we used to sleep in barns where the horses and cows were. And once, I was told here by a survivor that walked together with us that somebody offered me to escape--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you [? remember-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: --to hide SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me-- they told me, don't you remember that so-and-so offered you in this and that town? And I didn't want-- I didn't accept it. I said no. That sort of like, little by little, it came to my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell us that story, when you were given a chance to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave-- he gave me a chance to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was this person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just someone on the side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone on the side offered-- sawing us. And we walked sort of like in the groups, with the SS supervision. Some of 'em-- maybe some of 'em were accepting. I don't remember. But we were less and less. Some of them dropped. Maybe they went, and the-- and they accepted. I didn't want to accept it. So that was March, I would say, until-- I would say a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, what else can you remember about the march, other things happening to the other people marching with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marching and looking, visiting cities, [LAUGHS] towns in very uncomfortable conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing in particular that I-- no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my sister, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have other special friends that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we hold on together with some that we were together from my hometown. But everyone tried to stick together with somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, we were. It was just surprisingly, the way we-- the way we hold on-- amazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what did you do for warmth? What did you try to do for warmth, if anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had our clothing. And if we had some food to sustain us, and then the blankets. I don't remember if we got in the barn some blankets-- how we slept, whether anything. No, I don't remember, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular clothes with wooden shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lightweight clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been. Must have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was this? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a few weeks until we got into Flossenburg. Flossenburg was a-- a-- a camp where that--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think-- I understand that that must have been a political camp. And we were kept over there, I think, a week or so and shipped into a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before getting to the train, what do you remember about Flossenburg? What was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't do anything. We were-- we just were in a barracks, kept in a barracks. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: To lay on a bunk bed, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a hard surface, the bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a-- a Beautyrest mattress, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, did you meet other prisoners there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we must have socialized a little bit. But we couldn't do much. We were limited. If we were in a barrack, you couldn't go out from out the barrack and walk or socialize-- a little bit, maybe. But I don't have-- I have very little recollection about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened right when you got to Flossenburg? Was there a procedure for checking you in, getting you in to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- you're here. And that is. That's all. Accept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get new clothes, different--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you checked?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were in our clothes. We were in our clothes. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you checked medically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if somebody got sick. But I don't recall that I got sick. And we just kept and-- and waited to get the portion for the food and to-- and to continue to wait for the next day, what the next day will bring.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Flossenburg, were the conditions-- what were the conditions like compared to Neusalz? Was it very different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was quite a difference. In Neusalz, you knew that you have to go to work, to prepare yourself to go for a certain time, to go out and to be counted. In Flossenburg, you didn't have any work to do it. You just were laying around and waiting for what the next day will bring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about what that might be? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was nothing encouraging, just waiting, hoping. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you still maintained your sense of hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right, yeah. Keep on going.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the food at Flossenburg, the-- the hygiene conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm sure that we had, over there, some hygienes, some toilets to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we could keep clean. That's right. Maybe not as-- as showers. But we could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- yes, because I remember that we could face the next step, which was to go into the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So still talking about Flossenburg, were there men there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there were barracks with men over there. But not where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were never taken together with men. We were only woman with woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct, yes. Was with was my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any punishment or violence that you saw at that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't recall it. No, I don't recall. Maybe there was some who wanted to escape or what. But I don't recall. Myself, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any interaction with the Nazis there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't recall, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all, no. We just obeyed whatever we were told to do it. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, at that point, were you still with people that you knew from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we still have here now-- now the same people. They are-- it's a small--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Flossenburg, that did the same route that I did. We meet occasionally here now, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stuck together while you were in Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we could. We had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you-- after a couple of weeks, it was time you left Flossenburg. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flossenburg, they put us into train, because we were still-- as I see it now, we were still running away from the Russians. And the Russians already occupied some part of Germany. And we were running away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- sometimes in the train, we remembered that it was 10 days we were in the train without any water, anything, food or anything. So in those trains, when we-- they were stopping. And they didn't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because imagine, 10 days to be in a train and not to move, you've got to be a certain-- certain points where to go. You could have gone through all the whole state if you move. But 10 days. So finally, we wound up in Bergen-Belsen after 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to that train ride, tell me a little bit more about things you remember about those 10 days on the train. What did you talk about with the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the train, I didn't talk much. And I don't recollect much. I just know certain points that stopped in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: The going in, the being in without anything and being thirsty. And when the train stopped, we got out and licked the water from the gutters. That was something that sticks in my mind. And some of them got the typhus from it. I, fortunately, did not. But that was Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train, did-- did everyone survive on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so. Maybe they-- maybe they eliminated some bodies between when they stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I know very little. I remember very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you think of anything else that might have stuck out in your mind from the train ride, the 10-day train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did your sister fare on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We both kept together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was. So that was the sharing of everything. And that was the survival, the good and the bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train, did you sleep at all? Did you-- were you able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we could lay down, one on top of the other one, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eating?

speaker: interviewer
text: Eating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe we had some food in it. They got us in some-- maybe some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, as I remember. But I-- I just remembered the-- the beginning, going on the train, and then to get up. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the doors finally opened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ran out like animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We saw the Bergen-Belsen, the camp. And over there, we got into barracks. And of course, there was a time, maybe, when we had to go out to-- to-- to get the food, maybe once a day, or maybe in the morning. I don't remember this much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- this was what year? 1945? This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- yes, that was beginning '45. That must have been about March-- February, March '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the very first day in Bergen-Belsen? What happened that day? Can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, no. I just remember-- I-- I just remember the barracks to go, to be going from one barrack to another one and to see-- if to find anyone else who's there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: To pass the day. We didn't work over there. In Bergen-Belsen, we didn't work, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes some from other camps, from our-- from our hometown. But we stick together where-- at the camp-- at the barracks, that we were from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall cement. I don't recall bunk beds in Bergen-Belsen. I recall cement, yes. And clothes, that time we had-- the hygiene was very bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because I was full of lice in Bergen-Belsen. Itching. And if you-- we had a shower, we didn't have clean clothes to change. So the lice remained in the-- in the clothes, in the seams. That's where they congregate, the seams. I was a millionaire with lice. Very, very, very, very bad. Very bad infested, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did anyone-- did you go to an infirmary? Did anyone try and delouse you, take away the lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to get a change of clothes. But the change of clothes was lice too. It wasn't clean from the cleaner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, at that time, was very bad. She got typhus. And she was bad. I holded up much better than her, yes. I remember her-- her bones sticking out here from the-- very-- very thin, very-- very bad, yeah. She was in a-- in a worse condition than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like at Bergen-Belsen? What did you eat? [SIGH]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was survival, maybe, because it was out. You got the air. You got access to water. You couldn't live only on water. But at least once a day food, I think we had, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, at Bergen-Belsen, how did you deal with religion? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, you didn't know who you were even. Because in these conditions that we found ourselves, we did not think of-- of a Shabbat or religion or even-- maybe for-- from a-- as we used to say, God will help. OK, dear God, help us. Customarily, you say, oh-- or traditionally call it, help, help. That's it. But we-- I personally didn't lose hope, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, what sustained you at Bergen-Belsen? What or who sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I see it now, maybe sustained me Bergen-Belsen was my attitude with my work. And I would say, maybe it's the Red Cross packages, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were nice sandwiches, honey cake from the French that I got, that I could smuggle in between the [? waltzes ?] to-- to do my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe for us your job at Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Bergen-Belsen, I had no job. Bergen-Belsen, I was unemployed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what was the daily life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monotonous, just laying around and thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever comes. Whatever comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing any songs or tell any jokes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, talking about songs, there was one-- in Neusalz, there was one that used to sing 'My Yiddishe Momme.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's right. And I saw her in Brazil. She's gone by now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Toska Blaunstein, yeah. She really-- she was singing. And some of them tried to sing too. Yes, to make a little-- to lively up a little bit, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing the song, 'A Yiddishe Momme'?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [SINGING] My Yiddishe momme, this is a [YIDDISH]. My Yiddishe momme, I wish if you would be here now. [CRYING] I wish if my mother would live and to see, and survive, and to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and to know what life is, to have a little joy, both my parents, and my brothers too. But I remember my father very well. When he was in a really-- before we were separated, he said, those who will survive have to talk, not to be silent. I didn't listen to my father. I didn't practice this right away because I-- I didn't wanna put into my children hatreds. But it's-- I'm pleased that I opened now, and I-- and I can share and talk about this. And I wish if my parents would live through something and not to die in such a terrible way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, let me take you back to Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where you were with your sister. Did you speak of your parents together and wonder about what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe occasionally we did, maybe. Yeah, talk about home. Yes, with other ones, socializing a little bit. Yes, maybe we did. But I have a-- a-- I have a blackout to-- to recollect those mementos from-- maybe because they are not as-- maybe they were not important to me. I recollect some things that it must have been useful for me to keep me going. And to dwell on something that I am here and to cry, it's-- it hurts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But now, if I'd let it out, I share. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's my way.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Bergen-Belsen, did you interact with any Nazis there? Did you have any punishment or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I was not punished by anything, no. No, I was not punished, no. I was a good girl. I mind my work, and so did my sister. But my sister was-- and until today, she had a fear of a dog, which an SS man put a dog on her. And today, if she-- she won't come in a house if there is a dog in a house, especially a German shepherd. A small one, maybe, compromising. But she has a fear. I have no fear, no, no, because it wasn't put on me.
